base_method_text.title:
Transformers are SSMs: Generalized Models and Efficient Algorithms
  Through Structured State Space Duality
base_method_text.summary:
While Transformers have been the main architecture behind deep learning's
success in language modeling, state-space models (SSMs) such as Mamba have
recently been shown to match or outperform Transformers at small to medium
scale. We show that these families of models are actually quite closely
related, and develop a rich framework of theoretical connections between SSMs
and variants of attention, connected through various decompositions of a
well-studied class of structured semiseparable matrices. Our state space
duality (SSD) framework allows us to design a new architecture (Mamba-2) whose
core layer is an a refinement of Mamba's selective SSM that is 2-8X faster,
while continuing to be competitive with Transformers on language modeling.
base_method_text.main_contributions: The paper develops a theoretical framework called Structured State Space Duality (SSD) that connects structured state-space models (SSMs) and attention mechanisms through structured semiseparable matrices. It introduces a new hardware-efficient algorithm for computing SSMs and presents the Mamba-2 architecture, which refines the Mamba architecture, achieving competitive performance with Transformers on language modeling tasks while scaling more efficiently in sequence length.
base_method_text.methodology: The methodology involves establishing theoretical connections between SSMs and attention via matrix transformations and tensor contractions. It uses block decompositions of semiseparable matrices for efficient computation. The Mamba-2 architecture incorporates modifications like parallel parameter projections and extra normalization layers inspired by Transformer architectures.
base_method_text.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on language modeling tasks using the Pile dataset. The Mamba-2 architecture was evaluated on perplexity and zero-shot downstream evaluations, including LAMBADA, HellaSwag, PIQA, ARC-E, ARC-C, WinoGrande, and OpenbookQA. Efficiency benchmarks compared SSD against Mamba's scan implementation and FlashAttention-2. A multi-query associative recall task (MQAR) was also used to test long-range dependency handling.
base_method_text.limitations: The paper acknowledges a trade-off between expressivity and hardware efficiency in the SSD framework. The scalar-identity structure imposed on the A matrices in SSD is more restrictive than general diagonal SSMs, potentially limiting expressiveness. While Mamba-2 shows strong performance, there might be scenarios where more expressive SSMs or attention mechanisms are needed.
base_method_text.future_research_directions: Future research directions include refining structured matrix algorithms to improve performance on general diagonal SSMs, designing non-causal variants of Mamba, exploring the transfer of interpretability techniques from Transformers to SSMs, and investigating other structured masks within the structured masked attention (SMA) framework. Exploring hybrid architectures combining SSD layers with MLP and attention layers is also suggested.

add_method_texts.title: Gated Linear Attention Transformers with Hardware-Efficient Training
add_method_texts.summary:
Transformers with linear attention allow for efficient parallel training but
can simultaneously be formulated as an RNN with 2D (matrix-valued) hidden
states, thus enjoying linear-time inference complexity. However, linear
attention generally underperforms ordinary softmax attention. Moreover, current
implementations of linear attention lack I/O-awareness and are thus slower than
highly optimized implementations of softmax attention. This work describes a
hardware-efficient algorithm for linear attention that trades off memory
movement against parallelizability. The resulting implementation, dubbed
FLASHLINEARATTENTION, is faster than FLASHATTENTION-2 (Dao, 2023) as a
standalone layer even on short sequence lengths (e.g., 1K). We then generalize
this algorithm to a more expressive variant of linear attention with
data-dependent gates. When used as a replacement for the standard attention
layer in Transformers, the resulting gated linear attention (GLA) Transformer
is found to perform competitively against the LLaMA-architecture Transformer
(Touvron et al., 2023) as well recent linear-time-inference baselines such as
RetNet (Sun et al., 2023a) and Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2023) on moderate-scale
language modeling experiments. GLA Transformer is especially effective at
length generalization, enabling a model trained on 2K to generalize to
sequences longer than 20K without significant perplexity degradations. For
training speed, the GLA Transformer has higher throughput than a
similarly-sized Mamba model.
add_method_texts.main_contributions: This paper introduces FLASH LINEAR ATTENTION, a hardware-efficient algorithm for linear attention, and generalizes it to Gated Linear Attention (GLA). The GLA Transformer is shown to perform competitively against LLaMA-architecture Transformers and other linear-time inference baselines, particularly excelling in length generalization and recall-intensive tasks. The GLA Transformer also demonstrates higher training throughput than a similarly-sized Mamba model.
add_method_texts.methodology: The paper develops I/O-aware algorithms for linear attention, including a materialization and a non-materialization version, optimized for different training settings. It introduces a gated linear attention layer with a data-dependent gating mechanism and adapts FLASH LINEAR ATTENTION to this gated case. Secondary-level chunking is used to enable the use of half-precision matmuls, and a memory-efficient method for computing dαt is introduced.
add_method_texts.experimental_setup: The GLA Transformer is evaluated on moderate-scale language modeling benchmarks, with models trained with 340M/1.3B parameters on 15B/100B tokens, respectively, using the SlimPajama dataset and the Mistral tokenizer. Baselines include Transformer++, RetNet, and Mamba. Performance is measured using perplexity on Wikitext and LAMBADA, and accuracy on other downstream tasks. Length extrapolation is tested on SlimPajama and PG19 datasets. The models are trained using AdamW optimizer.
add_method_texts.limitations: The experiments were conducted on a respectable scale but were limited by compute resources, preventing larger-scale experiments. It is unclear how well GLA would scale to even larger models/datasets. The study focuses primarily on language modeling, and the impact of GLA on biased or harmful outputs of language models is not explored.
add_method_texts.future_research_directions: Future research directions include scaling GLA to larger models and datasets and applying GLA to other modalities, especially those with long-range dependencies. Further exploration of integrating different types of feature maps into GLA is also suggested.

base_experimental_code:
from mamba_ssm.modules.mamba2 import Mamba2
from mamba_ssm.modules.mamba_simple import Mamba

# Example usage of Mamba or Mamba2 module
# Forward pass
output = Mamba(d_model=512, d_state=16, d_conv=4, expand=2)(input_tensor)
output = Mamba2(d_model=512, d_state=128, d_conv=4, expand=2)(input_tensor)
base_experimental_info:
The Mamba-2 architecture incorporates modifications like parallel parameter projections and extra normalization layers inspired by Transformer architectures.

The benchmarks/benchmark_generation_mamba_simple.py file demonstrates the usage of MambaLMHeadModel for generation. It includes parameters such as model name, prompt, prompt length, generation length, temperature, top-k, top-p, min-p, and repetition penalty.
new_method:
Below is the outcome of step 3—a novel method we call the Gated Dual Structured State Space (G‑DS3) Transformer.

Overview of G‑DS3:
G‑DS3 is designed from the ground up by fusing key ideas from the Base Method’s Structured State Space Duality (SSD) framework with a data‐dependent gating mechanism inspired by the Gated Linear Attention (GLA) Transformer. This method is not just a tweak; it rethinks the state update and attention pathways by dynamically modulating the contribution of the state-space component based on the input context, thereby addressing inherent expressivity limitations without sacrificing hardware efficiency.

How We Reached G‑DS3:
1. Multiple issues with the Base Method were noted:
 • The SSD framework’s reliance on scalar–identity or similarly constrained A matrices improves hardware efficiency but limits the expressiveness of the state updates.
 • There is a trade‑off between efficiency and the network’s ability to model complex, data‐dependent relationships.
 • The Base Method could potentially benefit from more adaptive mechanisms to handle variable long-range dependencies.

2. From the Add Method, we selected the GLA Transformer’s approach:
 • The GLA Transformer introduces a data‐dependent gating mechanism that dynamically adjusts the computations in its linear attention layers.
 • This gating not only improves training throughput but also allows the network to modulate its behavior based on the input, offering a route to increase expressiveness while remaining efficient.

3. Our novel architecture, G‑DS3, draws inspiration from both sources:
 • At its core, G‑DS3 retains the structured state‐space formulation from the SSD framework. However, instead of imposing the fixed scalar–identity constraint on the state‐transition matrices, we introduce learnable, data‐dependent gating functions that modulate the state evolution.
 • In the G‑DS3 layer, the state update is given by a gated equation of the form:
  State_t = Gate(x_t, h_{t-1}) ⊙ f(h_{t-1}, x_t; A) + (1 − Gate(x_t, h_{t-1})) ⊙ h_{t-1},
 where:
  – f(⋅) represents the structured state‐space update inspired by SSD.
  – Gate(⋅) is a data‑dependent gating neural network (which may include lightweight MLP sub‑modules) outputting a dynamic weight per time step.
 • This gate is computed “I/O‑aware” using strategies from FLASH LINEAR ATTENTION, ensuring that the gating decision is efficient, leverages hardware‐friendly tensor operations, and minimizes memory movement.
 • The model also uses secondary-level chunking to further optimize compute and memory throughput on modern hardware.
 • By blending the strengths of rigorous state-space methods with adaptive gating, G‑DS3 can balance efficiency and expressivity. For segments where the input exhibits complex dependencies, the network can “open the gate” and allow richer state evolution, while in more predictable regions, it defaults to the efficient base update.

Advantages of G‑DS3:
 • Enhanced Expressivity: The dynamic gating mechanism alleviates the expressivity limitations inherent in fixed scalar-constraints by adapting the state evolution based on context.
 • Hardware Efficiency: By leveraging I/O-aware algorithms and chunking strategies from the GLA Transformer, G‑DS3 maintains high training throughput and inference speed, even on long sequences.
 • Flexibility: The architecture can naturally interpolate between behaving like a highly efficient linear operator and a more expressive, adaptive module—thus gracefully handling a range of sequence lengths and complexities.
 • Integration with Transformer-Principles: Additional normalization layers, MLP blocks, or multi-head variations can be integrated into the architecture, making it a standalone flexible alternative for language modeling and other sequence tasks.

In summary, the G‑DS3 Transformer represents a truly new integration: by merging structured state-space duality with data-dependent gating mechanisms optimized for hardware efficiency, it stands to overcome the expressivity limitations of the Base Method while retaining the efficiency and scalability needed for modern deep learning applications.
